"Is not this it in which my lord drinketh, and whereby indeed he divineth? ye have done evil in so doing." The witnesses note the cup of divination — the crux of Joseph's divining. On "Is not this it in which my lord drinketh," the grammatical voices note the cup. On "whereby indeed he divineth," interpreters weigh the crux. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the question — did Joseph practice divination, or was this only part of the feigned charge? Two readings: (a) the dominant reading — Joseph did not really practice divination (forbidden, Leviticus 19:26; Deuteronomy 18:10); the steward's words (and Joseph's own, v.15) were part of the stratagem and disguise — Joseph speaking as the Egyptians supposed of their great men (the cup-divination, hydromancy, being a known Egyptian art), keeping up the appearance of a heathen lord to conceal himself; the "divineth" a feigned pretense (or, that Joseph could "divine," i.e., discern, things, as by his God-given wisdom, the dreams, ch. 41); (b) a minority reading takes it that the cup was reputed (by the Egyptians) one of divination, the steward speaking of the common Egyptian practice without affirming Joseph used it; the witnesses are agreed that Joseph, a worshipper of the true God, did not really use divination (an abomination), but spoke (through the steward) in the character of an Egyptian magnate, part of the disguise and the test.

On "ye have done evil in so doing," the wesleyan-arminian and grammatical-historical note the charge: "ye have done evil in so doing" — the accusation of the theft; the feigned charge to alarm the brethren; the part of the stratagem (the test of their hearts). The verse displays the cup of divination crux.
